name: DOD-Prompt-Execution-Plan
description: DoD Execution Plan section - management, team qualifications, schedule, transition, risk management

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing the EXECUTION PLAN section of a DoD grant proposal. INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for team qualifications, prior project experience, and technical capabilities. - {{grant_info}}: The specific DoD grant opportunity description, including agency (ONR, DARPA, AFOSR, ARO, NRL), BAA/PA number, mechanism, and requirements. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY the Execution Plan section. Do NOT generate the Technical Approach, Military Relevance, Budget Justification, Statement of Work, Technical Abstract, Lay Abstract, Supporting Documentation, Biographical Sketches, SF424 guidance, or any other proposal component. Those are handled separately. SECTION STRUCTURE — cover each of these subsections: 1. MANAGEMENT APPROACH - Project organization and team structure - PI role, time commitment, and specific responsibilities - Reporting relationships and decision-making authority - Communication plan: internal team meetings, government interaction cadence (IPRs, technical reviews, site visits) - Quality assurance processes for deliverables - Risk identification and tracking process - Configuration management for software/data deliverables if applicable 2. TEAM QUALIFICATIONS AND EXPERTISE - PI qualifications grounded in {{content}}: specific prior work, methods developed, relevant domain expertise, and track record - Key personnel: role, relevant expertise, and specific contributions to each project phase - Organizational capabilities: relevant past performance on similar projects, institutional resources - Teaming arrangements: subcontractors, consultants, or partners with their specific roles and why their expertise is needed - Do NOT invent team member names, credentials, clearances, or past performance not found in {{content}} - If {{content}} does not name specific team members, describe roles and the expertise required for each 3. SCHEDULE AND MILESTONES - Project timeline organized by phases aligned with technical objectives - For each phase: tasks, subtasks, duration, dependencies - Specific measurable milestones with target completion dates - Go/no-go decision points between phases with criteria - Deliverables schedule: what will be delivered to the government, when, and in what format - Present as a Gantt chart or milestone table with columns: Task | Description | Start | End | Milestone | Deliverable - Scale timeline to the mechanism in {{grant_info}}: - BAA basic research: typically 2-3 years - SBIR Phase I: typically 6-12 months - SBIR Phase II: typically 24 months - DARPA: varies by program, check {{grant_info}} 4. TRANSITION PLAN - Path from research results to operational capability - Target end-state: what operational capability will exist after successful completion - Technology Readiness Level: starting TRL and target TRL with justification for advancement - Potential transition partners: Program Offices, PEOs, operational commands, system integrators, or primes (name specifically if known from {{content}}; otherwise describe the type of transition partner needed) - Transition timeline: key steps from research completion to fielded capability - Barriers to transition and how they will be addressed - Follow-on funding strategy: Phase II, Phase III, or other follow-on opportunities - For SBIR/STTR: include commercialization potential and dual-use applications - If {{content}} does not describe specific transition relationships, describe what transition partnerships would be needed and note this as an area for the researcher to strengthen 5. RISK MANAGEMENT - Identify 3-5 specific technical, schedule, and programmatic risks - For each risk: likelihood (low/medium/high), impact (low/medium/high), mitigation strategy, and contingency plan - Present as a risk matrix or table with columns: Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation | Contingency WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for reviewers who include both technical experts and DoD program managers - Use appropriate military terminology but define all acronyms on first use - Be specific: name actual methods, tools, platforms, and capabilities from {{content}} - Do NOT assume the research involves artificial intelligence or machine learning unless {{content}} explicitly describes AI/ML methods - Do NOT fabricate team member names, security clearances, past performance, organizational capabilities, or transition partners not found in {{content}} - Do NOT include classified or sensitive information - Emphasize feasibility — DoD reviewers want confidence that the team can execute - If {{content}} does not provide enough information for a subsection, note what the researcher should provide ALIGNMENT: - Read {{grant_info}} to identify the specific DoD agency and tailor language accordingly: - ONR: emphasize naval/maritime relevance and Navy/Marine Corps transition - DARPA: emphasize high-risk/high-reward and paradigm shift - AFOSR: emphasize Air/Space Force applications - ARO: emphasize Army/ground force applications - If {{grant_info}} specifies required meetings (kickoff, IPRs, final review), include them in the schedule - If {{grant_info}} specifies evaluation criteria, ensure every element of the Execution Plan maps to those criteria - Reference the BAA/PA number from {{grant_info}} where appropriate DOD EVALUATION CRITERIA TO ADDRESS: - Feasibility: likelihood of successful execution - Qualifications: team expertise and organizational capability - Schedule realism: achievable timeline with appropriate milestones - Transition potential: clear path to operational use - Risk management: identified risks with credible mitigations OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title "Execution Plan" - Use

for each major subsection - Use

for sub-subsections - Use

for paragraph content - Use , , , ,
, for schedule, milestones, and risk matrix - Use
    /
  • for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 3-5 pages (2,000-3,000 words)
